topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a lead weight. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes: "Congratulations on your publication in *The Chronicle*." My name, smeared across the byline, belonged to Marcus Thorne. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent office light suddenly too harsh, illuminating the fine tremor in my hand. I remembered the late nights, the meticulous research, the sheer, agonizing effort poured into that piece. Now, it was someone else’s victory lap. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow ache where pride used to reside. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and just stared at the wall, letting the silence of the empty room amplify the injustice.

[story 2]

I reread the digital confirmation three times, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. The realization hit me like a physical blow—my words, my voice, had been hijacked. It was a meticulously crafted piece of work, and it was being presented as a triumph of another soul. A sharp, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling a profound sense of deflation. Every carefully constructed ambition seemed to dissolve into meaningless dust. I picked up my phone, not to call anyone, but just to feel the weight of the device in my palm, a tangible representation of the wasted energy.

[story 3]

When the notification finally materialized, I didn't move immediately. I just sat there, perfectly still, the air thick and heavy around me. The initial shock quickly curdled into something sharp and corrosive. I traced the text on the screen with a fingertip, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure of ownership. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a harsh expulsion of air that tasted like ash. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the room. My footsteps were heavy, each stride a deliberate rejection of the situation. I wanted to scream, to dismantle the publication, but all that escaped was a tight, controlled set of clenched fists, the tension vibrating visibly in my arms.
